content.full_text:
SDGE San Diego Gas & Electric

PO. BOX 1831 • SAN DIEGO, CA 92112 • 619/699-5013

MARY J. WOOD
LAW DEPARTMENT
ASSISTANT GENERAL COUNSEL

October 13, 1992

FPPC
Oct 13 12:27 PM '92

FILE NO

CALIFORNIA FPPC
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Enclosed is a letter to you from Malcolm M. McCay, who has recently been employed by SDG&E. As you can see from his letter he was previously employed by the California Public Utilities Commission.

Both Mr. McCay and SDG&E want to be sure that Mr. McCay's duties are in compliance with the laws regulating former State employees.

Would you please give Mr. McCay an Advice letter pursuant to FPPC Bulletin, Volume 10, number 7, page 3. We understand that such letters are provided within 21 days after inquiry is made.

Please contact me or Mr. McCay if you need additional information in order to make your determination.

Very truly yours,

Mary J. Wood

MJW/dd

HALLABRILTR

SDGE San Diego Gas & Electric

Malcolm M. McCay
101 Ash St.
P.O. Box 1831
San Diego, CA 92112
(619) 699-5181
October 12, 1992


California Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     This letter will serve to request formal advice concerning my
duties under Government Code Sections 87400 et seq. I was
previously employed by the California Public Utilities Commission
("CPUC") as a Career Executive Appointment I.  My commission title
was Advisor to Commissioner John B. Ohanian.  My duties while at
the CPUC generally involved reviewing CPUC decisions and orders and
making recommendations to the commissioner regarding those
decisions.  On occasion, the CPUC would intervene as a party to a
Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC") proceeding.  One of
my duties while at the CPUC was to review the CPUC formal position
that it would present to FERC and suggest changes to the CPUC
position on FERC proceedings.  While at the CPUC, I did not
participate in any formal positions involving restructuring
proceedings for interstate pipelines, pursuant to FERC Order 636A.

     In my current position at San Diego Gas & Electric Company, I
will be acting as the lead staff person representing the Company in
several company restructuring cases pursuant to FERC Order 636.
These include restructuring dockets for Pacific Gas Transmission
Company, Kern River Transmission Company and Mojave Pipeline
Company. These proceedings are in separate dockets from FERC Order
636.  SDG&E is one party to the restructuring proceeding, along
with several other parties.  The CPUC has also intervened as a
party in these proceedings.  All parties to these proceedings are
on equal standing before FERC. It is important to note that the
CPUC has no jurisdiction in this proceeding.  It is merely a party
to the proceeding.  This is a matter under federal jurisdiction.
Pursuant to FERC Order 636A, all parties are required to hold
meetings and reach agreements on various issues involving the
interstate pipelines.  Thus, as a part of the FERC proceeding, I
will be required to communicate and interact with the CPUC staff,
since the CPUC is party to the proceeding.  In light of this
situation, let me present some specific situations and questions:

1. In a settlement conference where several different interveners are present, can I ask why the CPUC holds a certain position in the PGT Order 636 Restructuring Case? May I discuss the issue with the intent of influencing the CPUC's position?

2. In the PGT Order 636 Restructuring Case, may I meet separately with the CPUC's staff to discuss issues in the proceeding?

3. In a FERC proceeding which goes to hearing in Washington D.C., may I assist our counsel in preparing cross examination questions for the CPUC witnesses?

4. In a FERC proceeding such as the PGT Order 636 Restructuring Case, may I call a CPUC staff person and inquire about a CPUC position?

5. In a FERC proceeding which goes to hearing, may I file rebuttal testimony to the CPUC position?

     Please contact me if you have further questions or if you require any additional facts to render your opinion.


Sincerely,

[signature]
Malcolm M. McCay

Wood, Assistant General Counsel at San Diego Gas & Electric (SDG&E), to Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel of the California Public Utilities Commission (FPPC). The letter is dated October 13, 1992. My duties while at the CPUC generally involved reviewing CPUC decisions and orders and making recommendations to the commissioner regarding those decisions. On occasion, the CPUC would intervene as a party to a Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC") proceeding. One of my duties while at the CPUC was to review the CPUC formal position that it would present to FERC and suggest changes to the CPUC position on FERC proceedings. While at the CPUC, I did not participate in any formal positions involving restructuring proceedings for interstate pipelines, pursuant to FERC Order 636A. In my current position at San Diego Gas & Electric Company, I will be acting as the lead staff person representing the Company in several company restructuring cases pursuant to FERC Order 636. These include restructuring dockets for Pacific Gas Transmission Company, Kern River Transmission Company and Mojave Pipeline Company. These proceedings are in separate dockets from FERC Order 636. SDG&E is one party to the restructuring proceeding, along with several other parties. The CPUC has also intervened as a party in these proceedings. All parties to these proceedings are on equal standing before FERC. It is important to note that the CPUC has no jurisdiction in this proceeding, it is merely a party to the proceeding. This is a matter under federal jurisdiction. Pursuant to FERC Order 636A, all parties are required to hold meetings and reach agreements on various issues involving the interstate pipelines.
